title: Toxicological Profile for Ionizing Radiation
author_or_org: Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
pages_or_sections: ATSDR PDF p. 48; mirrored in NCBI Bookshelf lines 118-120
strengths: Best accessible matched resident-population / first-day death series for both cities; explicit first-day versus subsequent 4-month distinction; official U.S. government source.
limitations: Hiroshima line explicitly says civilian population; Nagasaki line says resident population rather than civilian population; notes undercount of military personnel and foreign workers.
notes: Core source for baseline civilian first-day direct deaths and resident-civilian denominators.

title: The Army Air Forces in World War II, Volume V: MATTERHORN to Nagasaki, Chapter 23
author_or_org: U.S. Army Air Forces official history (web edition hosted by HyperWar)
pages_or_sections: Web edition lines 240-271 (pp. 722-725 in the original volume)
strengths: Explicit Hiroshima military count ('6,769 soldiers out of 24,158'); useful Japanese official and USSBS total casualty context for both cities.
limitations: Hiroshima military claim is embedded in narrative rather than a fully transparent roster; Nagasaki section gives broad total minimum dead but not a clean military split.
notes: Main source for Hiroshima military numerator and denominator.

title: The Atomic Bombing of Hiroshima, August 6, 1945
author_or_org: U.S. Department of Energy, Office of Scientific and Technical Information
strengths: Official U.S. history page giving a simple civilian / soldier split for Hiroshima on the attack day.
limitations: Round figures; not a direct casualty reconstruction; not clearly tied to a specific postwar enumeration method.
notes: Used only as a sensitivity bound for Hiroshima exposure denominators.

title: The Path from the Atomic Bombing to Reconstruction of Hiroshima
strengths: Official Japanese local-government statement on day-of population including military and commuters; also provides later end-1945 death estimate.
limitations: Not a clean civilian/military split for numerator construction; end-1945 death figure mixes immediate and delayed mortality.
notes: Used as context and for explaining why broad totals exceed same-day direct-death baselines.

title: Damage Caused by the Atomic Bombing
pages_or_sections: Official page text as captured in search snippet; also reflected in official leaflet materials
strengths: Official Nagasaki city presentation of population, deaths, and injuries through end of December 1945.
limitations: End-1945 totals mix immediate and delayed mortality; no clean civilian/military split.
notes: Used as contextual source and as a wider civilian exposure bound.

title: Bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki – 1945
author_or_org: Atomic Heritage Foundation / National Museum of Nuclear Science & History
pages_or_sections: Hiroshima section lines 45-47; Nagasaki section lines 80-82
strengths: Convenient summary source for broad city composition on bombing day, especially Nagasaki's 263,000 total including 9,000 Japanese soldiers.
limitations: Secondary museum summary rather than a primary casualty reconstruction; immediate-death figures are broad and not status-specific.
notes: Used mainly for Nagasaki military-presence denominator and Hiroshima exposure sensitivity context.

title: Counting the dead at Hiroshima and Nagasaki
author_or_org: Alex Wellerstein, Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists
strengths: Excellent historiographic map of why estimates differ: time horizon, population base, omission of military or Korean workers, and postwar methodological disputes.
limitations: Secondary synthesis, not a primary casualty registry; some figures are reported from newspaper summaries or later reconstructions.
notes: Used to explain disagreement, to support Hiroshima first-day 40,000-50,000 context, and to flag omission of military victims in some American studies.

title: Hiroshima and Nagasaki Bombing Survey, Chapter X: Total Casualties
author_or_org: Avalon Project (reproducing Manhattan Engineer District / postwar report text)
pages_or_sections: Lines 15-18 and nearby text in web capture
strengths: Early official U.S. postwar totals with pre-raid population baselines and deaths/injuries for both cities.
limitations: Not first-day direct-only; totals blend immediate and delayed deaths; no clean civilian/military split.
notes: Used to show why early U.S. totals are lower than later Japanese-led end-1945 estimates.

title: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
strengths: Provides the only quickly accessible same-day Nagasaki military-deaths split in the searched source set.
limitations: Tertiary and not preferred; should be replaced with a primary or archival source if this bundle is revised.
notes: Used only for the Nagasaki military-deaths best estimate and clearly down-weighted in confidence.
